sentences.text: OK.
1944 into 1945-- what exactly was it like in hiding?
Anything that you can remember that you haven't already said.
OK, what do I remember?
I remember air raids, lots of air raids.
I remember-- when we were hiding in the second apartment house, I remember not going out on the street.
There were, I would say, good couple of months-- two to three months-- where I wasn't out on the street at all.
Because at that point, A, we were hiding.
B, it was very dangerous.
It was fighting on the streets.
The Hungarian soldiers, the Germans, and the Russians.
And the Russians were zeroing in on and really advancing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were hearing the gunfights.
We were hearing the air raids.
We were hearing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things that were extremely scary, especially scary for a young kid.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Memories of being scared also are very vivid, because my father wasn't always around.
As I said earlier, he was coming and going and keeping ahead of them, because he knew he was an able-bodied man.
And if anybody finds him, he'll be taken immediately.
Whether it was too late to be taken to the camps, at that point-- which I think it was-- or whether he was going to be just plain shot.
One doesn't know.
We also knew that as time was running out on the Germans, they were trying to just shoot people on the streets.
And not--
This is late '44, early '45.
That's the time frame here.
Things were really getting very tight for the Germans.
I mean, there were parts of Europe already liberated at that point.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Remember, Hungary was the last one they occupied and the last one they were giving up, and Budapest was their stronghold.
So they were demolishing Budapest.
And were trying to-- they were still very much intent on killing as many Jews as they can get their hands on.
So we were very much aware.
We were very much aware as we got into February and March that they were no-- of '45-- that they no longer took them to the trains and taking them to the various concentration camps.
But instead, marching them down to the Danube and shooting them into the Danube.
This is a memory that I clearly remember that people were talking about.
As it turns out, we were liberated on April 4th of '45.
And we subsequently learned from the superintendent of this apartment house where we were hiding that we were supposed to have been marched down to the Danube and shot down into the Danube.
Just boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, straight down.
And we knew that this has happened.
The Danube, the famous blue Danube wasn't really blue.
You could see red-- all the blood of people who were shot in there.
So it was a very, very, very trying an experience to go through.
Memories were a lot of fighting of the people who were-- as I said, about 15 people in one room.
And in other rooms, other 15 people.
A lot of fighting, people having a great difficulty getting along.
These were total strangers.
Difficulties of access to the bathroom.
No food, very clear memories of no food, because this was war.
And at the end of the war-- and this was winter.
So it's winter, and war, and very little food to go around.
And if you got any food, you paid a very high price for it.
And it usually came through the superintendent of the building.
That's the recollection that I do have.
I have recollections of my sister and my mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being the people, obviously, to whom I was looking up to.
And wondering as to what was going to happen, and waiting my clue and my cue as to what was happening from them.
I have memories of having a favorite doll, and that doll came with me.
That was the only toy that came with me.
And if you recall, I said we left in the middle of the night our original apartment building, and then we went into hiding in this building.
And that doll went through as much with me as I went through basically.
And that was the only toy that I remember having.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember people not really doing anything.
I have no recollection in this setup of people playing cards.
For example, as previously I mentioned, we were playing cards to kill time.
I have absolutely no recollection of that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have clear recollection of the people in the-- in this room.
And everybody on everybody else's nerve.
And everybody on everybody else's back literally.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What-- how what we knew what went on, we only knew as to what went on the outside if the super came in or if somebody like my father came in who was carefully watching as to what was going on.
But we knew that things were getting tougher and tougher, and the fighting was getting closer and closer.
That's the recollection and that's the memory that I do have.
No, I have no recollection of other children.
I really don't.
I remember always being with adults and no children.
Which is-- as I reflected upon it earlier-- I don't know how a child really can go through that.
That really must have been very, very difficult.
I always do remember being among adults and never among children.
I remember being hungry.
I do remember being hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do remember the rationing of the food at that point.
I do remember a lot of the crying that my mother was doing.
I do remember that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I do remember the just general, untenable situation.
That's-- that's my recollection.
You could see the street.
And you could see usually empty streets, or you could see groups of people being marched with soldiers with machine guns on their shoulder in the front of the group and in the back of the group.
And they were primarily women and young people.
There were very few men.
That is clearly the recollection that I have.
I don't think there were too many men left.
I really don't.
And what's interesting later that I had very few Jewish friends after the war who remained a nuclear family-- that's primarily a man around.
The men were more readily taken and never coming back from the camps than the women were.
Somehow, many of the women survived with the kids.
But the men were not there.
So that really showed that they were after able-bodied men.
And they had not much use or they didn't want to waste their time on-- at that point.
At that point, they must have already taken everybody.
I mean, out in the countryside and in the smaller cities, the Jews were already gone.
I mean, in span of about three months, they took thousands of them away.
And any of the documentaries that exist attest to this, because they were just emptied out-- the small towns, the Jewish population of the small town.
So that being in the large city in many ways was a protection.
Of course it didn't mean that you were protected completely, but it certainly was a protection.
I remember hearing lots of talk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember hearing hush-hush as to what was going on in the countryside and in the smaller cities.
Yes, I do remember discussions of those.
And of course, I also remember discussions of what was taking place in Budapest.
And how they were rounding people up, and how they were getting rid of them.
I mean, I have a pretty strong recollection of-- of the fear and of the knowledge that you didn't know what was going to happen to you.
I think that is the major point that one wants to make-- that fear in hiding can do strange things to people.
Absolutely.
When I first saw Anne Frank-- the hiding in that small place up in the attic seemed extremely familiar to me with the families forced in together.
Except the point of time that I remember, things-- the last place where we were hiding, things were really not as quote, 'pleasant,' as they seemed in the 'Diary of Anne Frank' play.
Of course, the diary itself-- the book is a whole other story.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very little furnishing in the-- in this room that I recall.
I recall being on the floor on blankets and spending an awful lot of time on the floor.
That's-- that's parquet floor is what the recollection is.
No carpeting.
Parquet floor.
That's very clearly the recollection.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the overriding memory is the fighting.
People simply couldn't get along.
And I guess couldn't get along because everybody was so afraid.
And these were total strangers.
The first place we were with people that we knew, and people did get along.
Not to say that there were no occasional, you know, problems, frictions.
But that's-- when you-- all those people live under one roof-- that's inevitable.
But the other one is a totally different recollection.
My sister was a very capable teenager who-- this one memory where she saved herself is very clear.
And as she was very protective-- I remember her being very protective of me and being very protective of my mother as well.
That's a clear recollection also.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was at a point where-- which is a hard age for girls to be-- 13, 14.
But she had no time to be a teenager in the sense of an American teenager of today.
That-- that was not in the cards.
Life was very difficult and her upbringing and the things that we went through dealt her a very different deck of cards.
So that her way of surviving-- when you have to think about nothing but how are you going to survive-- plays into your life then and later on very differently.
The instinct of survival is really very strong in people.
And that has been documented in many, many cases-- both of the Holocaust and other calamities-- how people tried to survive.
And how young people who survive is also-- I think is even more interesting.
Because these are strictly instincts.
They don't have the training, and the experience, and anything to look back on.
They really have themselves to rely on as far as the whole issue of survival is concerned.
The memories extend from-- for example, her finding the soldier who was going to be taking us from the original apartment house to where we ended up.
I mean, to think that a young girl can go out, and have the presence of mind, and the ability to deal with a major situation.

And find somebody and convince that person-- with money, obviously-- that he should take these three women to another part of the city and to deliver them-- is a pretty mature thing for a 13, 14-year-old girl to do.
Other memories are that if food came in, she would let me have it instead of her having it.
If there was some food that became available, she would always have the smaller portion and let me have the larger portion. trying to be a companion to me.
Trying to calm me down.
Trying to play with me.
But of course, it really wasn't circumstance for playing but still trying to preserve some sense of normalcy, I guess.
What a little kid would expect to be normal.
I have two very distinct memories of food, which I guess maybe we should talk about now.
Because that's something that my own daughters know, which has to do with A, fish, and B, corn muffins and corn bread.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that has to do with the fact that after the war-- and I'm now jumping-- after the liberation [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was really no food.
And we can talk about the whole liberation process, but I think we should talk about these two issues.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother was trying to scrounge up food, and the only thing that was available was corn meal.
So she was baking cornmeal, and doing all kinds of things with cornmeal, whatever she could think of to do.
To this day, I will not eat a corn muffin.
I will not eat cornbread.
I will not eat anything that is made of cornmeal in any shape, form.
I just won't touch it.
The other thing is that I ate no fish.
I was brought up with no fish in my house after the war, because my mother-- remember, we-- Hungary's is a landlocked country.
Landlocked country, no access to the sea.
So there was no seafood.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is a large lake in the middle of the country, and you will get freshwater fish from there.
But much of the fish came what was fished out from the Danube.
And because of the memories associated with the Danube-- that is people being shot in there-- my mother simply could not bring herself to bring in fish into the house.
So I grew up having never tasted fish.
The first time I had fish was in the early '60s when my husband gently started getting me onto trying fish.
And I-- we got to the point of me having some fish in restaurants.
But I, myself, did not start actually cooking fish until the mid to late '80s, which I think is quite a significant [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] occurrence.
And I'm still very careful as to what fish I will eat.
Let's go back to the hiding.
My mother was a very strong woman, very strong.
Think back when she held the family together, when my father was in this forced labor camp situation, and she was the one who was running the law office with this Christian friend of my father.
She was a woman who has never been working outside of the house.
She was a very highly educated lady.
And very much of a lady who devoted her life to her family and to her children.
But very much of the world-- she was not a woman who has never understood what the world was all about.
In that context, remember, that she also was a survivor-- was a survivor of World War I as a young woman and got herself into World War II.
She was a strong individual who could do just about anything she needed to do.
And she always knew what it was that she had to do to survive and to make sure that her family survived.
Yes.
Yes.
The concept of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just getting us to that hiding place, I think, is a very important fact that she had accomplished.
Getting us organized and getting us there.
And then while we were in this place, to keeping us together and to using her brain to use these protection letters for our survival.
I think was quite a feat under the circumstance.
Now she-- her lifeline was my father.
And maintaining that lifeline to him was always extremely, extremely important.
But she did manage.
She did manage in the sense of finding whatever she needed to find.
If it was food that she needed to find for us, she managed to find us food.
If it was finding someone who was going to connect with my father, she managed to find someone who was going to connect with my father.
And this always seemed amazing to me.
They were always in touch.
Wherever he was hiding, wherever he was running, they were always in touch.
And that's quite a feat.
He was going into abandoned buildings.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] He was going into places where he knew that he would be safe for awhile.
He was going in to-- other peoples that he knew.
He was contacting his friends that he could get in touch with-- non-Jews.
And periodically, he would come into this hiding place where we were when he thought he was safe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he was out on the streets as much as he was in.
It's quite an amazing feat.
I mean, to me he is-- he was an unbelievable individual.
The whole-- if anybody knew how to survive, it was him.
And how to manage under those circumstances.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if you want to go on as to how the liberation happened, but I think that's an interesting story as well.
I think it was a mystery more than anything else.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very close to my father until the day died.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had a very special relationship with his daughters, both daughters.
There was a lot of real-- at that point, it wasn't admiration.
It was awe, I think, more than anything else.
The fact that he would disappear, and then he would come right back.
The fact that-- that I was able to see him.
And I understood that times were very different, obviously.
I mean, you have to understand the interplay of war going on, bombs being dropped, fighting going on on the streets.
You having to wear the yellow Star of David as a little kid.
At the end, not being able to get out on the streets, because it was too dangerous.
And-- and you just absolutely were not allowed.
It was A, dangerous, B, you were not allowed.
And being in a situation with 15 other people in a room, and you being hungry more often than not.

It's a pretty amazing confluence of events and circumstances that make for somebody's understanding or trying to understand, and the memories that that leaves, and how one develops as a result of these factors.
It also creates for a very tight family, a nuclear family that is very tight and in many ways very unique as I later on learned in life.
They were looking at me that you were the only one who has both parents there.
If you were a Jewish kid and you had both of your parents, that was unheard of during the time when I was growing up after the war.
I think all I wanted is the shooting to stop.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all I wanted is to quote, 'be able to go home.' I think that's all I wanted.
That-- that's-- and of course, I was supposed to start first grade.
I was supposed to be in first grade in September of '44, which never happened.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a very clear recollection of that also.
You don't go-- you don't not go to school because you are a Jew.
You don't go to school because nobody else is going to school because there is a war going on.
And there is simply nothing that works.
There is no normalcy.
I mean, I think the memory that I've got and what we need to think about is total abnormalcy and nothing the way that one would expect.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even though-- even when I survived, I survived-- and that was out of the normal, because most people in my situation did not survive.
So surviving and surviving under the circumstances was out of the ordinary.
And surviving with my parents and my sister was also out of the ordinary.
I know that there was always a lot of talk as to what happened to my cousin, the cousin who was killed at Theresienstadt, that's where-- either Bergen-Belsen or to Theresienstadt.
We were never knew-- sure which one she ended up at.
There were sightings of her at both, so we obviously never knew what happened.
So that you knew that all of a sudden you had a favorite cousin, and that favorite cousin is no longer there.
Now, this was a cousin who was about a dozen years older than I was.
And there was all-- again, somebody else who babied me, and she disappeared.

So it's really a confluence of the out of the normal, out of the ordinary, watching buildings being bombed, watching people that you know and you love disappear, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] knowing that something awful is happening, but not really knowing what is happening.
I mean, how much does a little kid really understand?
Knowing that something terrible is going on.
And always looking at your mother who is always worrying and always holding onto you like your-- her life is depending on you.
And that's exactly what she was doing.
OK, we're about done with this tape.
Who is that?
Is there somebody wants to come in?
